title: Contact Dermatitis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Dermatology, Primary Care
objectives: Differentiate irritant contact dermatitis from allergic contact dermatitis
Understand the Type IV hypersensitivity mechanism of ACD
Identify common contact allergens (nickel, fragrance, preservatives, topical medications)
Apply patch testing principles for diagnosis
Implement appropriate treatment and allergen avoidance strategies

guidelineReferences.name: ESCD Guideline for Diagnostic Patch Testing 2015
Contact Dermatitis Practice Parameter 2015
guidelineReferences.citation: Fonacier L, et al. Contact Dermatitis: A Practice Parameter. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2015

guidelineReferences.name: JACI Practice 2024: Recognizing and Managing ACD

vignette.text:
A 34-year-old hairdresser presents with a 6-month history of itchy, red, cracked skin on her hands that worsens at work and improves on vacation. She notes the rash started after she began using a new line of hair dyes at her salon.

Past Medical History: Mild atopic dermatitis as a child (resolved), Seasonal allergies

Medications: Cetirizine PRN

Allergies: NKDA

skin.hands: Bilateral dorsal hands with erythema, scaling, fissuring, and lichenification; web spaces affected; fingertips with cracks
skin.distribution: Sharply demarcated at wrists, sparing palms
skin.other: No lesions elsewhere, no vesicles currently

diagnostics.initial_workup: Clinical diagnosis suspected - patch testing planned

questions.stem: This patient has chronic hand dermatitis with occupational exposure.
questions.question: What are the two main types of contact dermatitis?

options.text: There is only one type of contact dermatitis
Both types are IgE-mediated
Irritant dermatitis requires sensitization

IRRITANT CONTACT DERMATITIS (ICD): 80% of cases; Non-immunologic; Direct skin damage from irritants; No prior sensitization needed; Anyone can develop with sufficient exposure; ALLERGIC CONTACT DERMATITIS (ACD): 20% of cases; Type IV (delayed) hypersensitivity; Requires prior sensitization; Only sensitized individuals react; Specific to particular allergens

questions.explanation: Two main types: IRRITANT CONTACT DERMATITIS (ICD): Accounts for ~80% of contact dermatitis. Non-immunologic mechanism - direct chemical damage to skin. No prior sensitization required - can occur on first exposure with sufficient irritant. Dose-dependent - higher concentration/duration increases severity. Common irritants: soaps, detergents, solvents, acids, alkalis, wet work. ALLERGIC CONTACT DERMATITIS (ACD): Accounts for ~20% of cases. Type IV (T-cell mediated) delayed hypersensitivity. Requires prior sensitization (initial exposure leads to memory T-cells). On re-exposure, memory T-cells trigger inflammation. Specific allergens - ~3000 known contact allergens. This patient likely has ACD given occupational exposure to hair dyes and improvement with avoidance.

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You want to understand why this patient developed ACD to hair dye.
questions.question: What is the mechanism of allergic contact dermatitis?

options.text: Complement-mediated reaction
Antibody-dependent cytotoxicity
IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity

TYPE IV HYPERSENSITIVITY (Delayed): Two phases: (1) SENSITIZATION (10-14 days): Hapten (small molecule <500 Da) penetrates skin, binds proteins (haptenization), Langerhans cells present to T-cells in lymph nodes, Memory T-cells formed; (2) ELICITATION (24-72 hours): Re-exposure to allergen, Memory T-cells in skin recognize hapten, Release cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α), Inflammation develops 24-48h after contact

questions.explanation: ACD is Type IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity: SENSITIZATION PHASE: Initial exposure to allergen (hapten - small molecule <500 Daltons). Hapten penetrates stratum corneum and binds skin proteins (haptenization). Langerhans cells (dendritic cells in epidermis) capture haptenated proteins. Migration to regional lymph nodes, presentation to naïve T-cells. Clonal expansion of allergen-specific memory T-cells (CD4+ and CD8+). This takes 10-14 days for strong allergens. ELICITATION PHASE: Re-exposure to the same allergen. Memory T-cells in skin recognize haptenated proteins. Release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ). Mast cell and macrophage activation. Clinical dermatitis develops 24-72 hours after exposure. This is why hair dye reactions occur after use, not immediately.

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has chronic changes on her hands.
questions.question: What are the clinical features of contact dermatitis?

options.text: ACUTE: Erythema, edema, vesicles, bullae, weeping, crusting; Pruritus prominent; Sharp borders corresponding to contact area; SUBACUTE: Less vesiculation, more scaling; CHRONIC: Lichenification (thickened skin with accentuated lines), Scaling, fissuring (cracking), Hyperkeratosis; Distribution often reveals cause: Hands (occupational), Ears/neck (jewelry), Face (cosmetics), Feet (shoes)
Always presents with vesicles
Urticaria and angioedema
Distribution is random

questions.explanation: Clinical features vary by chronicity: ACUTE ACD: Erythema, edema, vesicles/bullae, weeping, crusting. Intense pruritus. Sharp borders at contact site. Classic example: Poison ivy - linear streaks of vesicles. SUBACUTE: Less vesiculation, more erythema and scaling. Transition phase. CHRONIC: Lichenification (thickened skin, accentuated markings), Scaling, fissuring (cracks), Hyperkeratosis. This patient shows chronic changes. DISTRIBUTION CLUES: Hands: Occupational (this patient - hairdresser), Earlobes/neck: Nickel jewelry, Periorbital: Nail polish (touching face), cosmetics, Feet: Shoe materials, Wrists: Watch bands, bracelets. ICD vs ACD clinically similar but ICD often affects multiple workers, ACD affects only sensitized individuals.

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! questions.stem: You suspect hair dye allergy in this hairdresser.
questions.question: What are the most common contact allergens?

options.text: Food allergens cause most ACD

Top allergens (NACDG data): NICKEL (most common - jewelry, belt buckles); FRAGRANCE MIX (cosmetics, personal care); BALSAM OF PERU (cross-reacts with fragrances, foods); NEOMYCIN (topical antibiotic); PRESERVATIVES: Formaldehyde releasers, Methylisothiazolinone (MI/MCI); Hair dye: PARA-PHENYLENEDIAMINE (PPD) - this patient's likely culprit; Rubber accelerators (gloves); Topical corticosteroids; Propylene glycol
Latex protein is the top allergen
Only nickel causes ACD

questions.explanation: Most common contact allergens: NICKEL: Most common overall (14% of patch-tested patients). Found in jewelry, belt buckles, buttons, coins, cell phones. FRAGRANCE MIX: In cosmetics, soaps, detergents, personal care products. BALSAM OF PERU: Cross-reacts with fragrances, flavorings, some foods. NEOMYCIN: Topical antibiotic - cross-reacts with other aminoglycosides. PRESERVATIVES: Formaldehyde and formaldehyde-releasing preservatives, Methylisothiazolinone (MI) and Methylchloroisothiazolinone (MCI) - rising allergen. PARA-PHENYLENEDIAMINE (PPD): Hair dye allergen - this patient's likely cause! Also in temporary/henna tattoos. May cross-react with PABA, sulfonamides, azo dyes. RUBBER ACCELERATORS: Carbamates, thiurams, mercaptobenzothiazole - in gloves. TOPICAL MEDICATIONS: Neomycin, bacitracin, and even topical corticosteroids!

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You decide to perform patch testing on this patient.
questions.question: How is patch testing performed and interpreted?

options.text: GOLD STANDARD for ACD diagnosis; PROCEDURE: Allergens in chambers applied to upper back; Occlusion for 48 hours; Readings at 48h AND 72-96h (crucial for delayed reactions); INTERPRETATION: Negative (-), Doubtful (?+), Weak positive (+), Strong positive (++), Extreme positive (+++); CRESCENDO pattern (reaction increases 48→96h) = allergic; DECRESCENDO pattern (fades) = irritant; Must correlate with clinical relevance
Positive if immediate wheal forms
Only test one allergen at a time
Read only at 24 hours

questions.explanation: Patch Testing: GOLD STANDARD for diagnosing ACD. PROCEDURE: Allergens prepared in petrolatum or aqueous vehicle. Applied in small chambers (Finn chambers or TRUE test) to upper back. Occluded for 48 hours. Patient avoids wetting/sweating. READINGS: First reading at 48 hours (when patches removed). Second reading at 72-96 hours - ESSENTIAL to distinguish allergic from irritant reactions. GRADING: (-) Negative, (?) Doubtful/questionable, (+) Weak positive (erythema, papules), (++) Strong positive (vesicles), (+++) Extreme (bullous). KEY DISTINCTION: Allergic reactions show CRESCENDO pattern (increase from 48→96h). Irritant reactions show DECRESCENDO pattern (fade). CLINICAL RELEVANCE: Must correlate positive test with patient's exposure history and dermatitis location. Definite, probable, possible, or past relevance.

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patch test shows ++ reaction to PPD at 96 hours.
questions.question: How do you determine clinical relevance of a positive patch test?

options.text: Any positive patch test is relevant
Relevance cannot be determined

CLINICAL RELEVANCE categories: DEFINITE: Positive test + exposure confirmed + dermatitis at contact site + improves with avoidance; PROBABLE: Positive test + likely exposure + compatible distribution; POSSIBLE: Positive test + possible exposure; PAST: Explains previous episode, not current; NOT RELEVANT: No connection to current dermatitis; This patient: PPD positive + hairdresser + hand dermatitis + improves on vacation = DEFINITE RELEVANCE
Only definite relevance matters

questions.explanation: Determining Clinical Relevance: A positive patch test only shows sensitization - must establish if it explains the patient's dermatitis! RELEVANCE CATEGORIES: DEFINITE RELEVANCE: Positive patch test to allergen, Confirmed current exposure to that allergen, Dermatitis distribution matches exposure, Improves with avoidance and/or recurs with rechallenge. PROBABLE: Likely exposure based on history, compatible distribution. POSSIBLE: Possible but unconfirmed exposure. PAST RELEVANCE: Explains previous dermatitis episode, not current. UNKNOWN: Cannot establish exposure. NOT RELEVANT: Sensitized but no connection to current presentation. This patient: PPD (+) reaction + hairdresser using hair dyes + hand dermatitis + improves on vacation = DEFINITE RELEVANCE. Counseling: Must avoid PPD and cross-reactive substances.

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is CLINICAL RELEVANCE categories: DEFINITE: Positive test + exposure confirmed + dermatitis at contact site + improves with avoidance; PROBABLE: Positive test + likely exposure + compatible distribution; POSSIBLE: Positive test + possible exposure; PAST: Explains previous episode, not current; NOT RELEVANT: No connection to current dermatitis; This patient: PPD positive + hairdresser + hand dermatitis + improves on vacation = DEFINITE RELEVANCE because Determining Clinical Relevance: A positive patch test only shows sensitization - must establish if it explains the patie

questions.stem: You want to differentiate ICD from ACD.
questions.question: How do irritant and allergic contact dermatitis differ?

options.text: ICD: Non-immune; Occurs on first exposure; Dose-dependent (stronger irritant = faster reaction); Anyone can develop; Often multiple workers affected; Burning/stinging > itch; Hands most common. ACD: Type IV immune; Requires sensitization; Small amounts can trigger; Only sensitized individuals; Often single worker affected; Itch predominant; Distribution matches allergen contact. OVERLAP common; histology often indistinguishable
ICD and ACD are clinically identical
Only ICD affects hands
ICD is always more severe

questions.explanation: Key differences between ICD and ACD: IRRITANT (ICD): Non-immunologic direct damage, Can occur on first exposure, Dose-dependent (concentration × duration), Anyone with sufficient exposure can develop, Often multiple workers affected in occupational setting, Burning/stinging sensation often > itch, Hands most commonly affected (wet work), Reaction often immediate or within hours. ALLERGIC (ACD): Type IV hypersensitivity, Requires prior sensitization (days to years of exposure), Small amounts can trigger once sensitized, Only affects sensitized individuals, May be only one worker in same exposure setting, Itch is predominant symptom, Distribution matches allergen contact pattern, Delayed reaction (24-72 hours). IMPORTANT: Clinical appearance and histology often indistinguishable. Patch testing required to diagnose ACD. Many patients have BOTH (damaged barrier from ICD allows penetration → ACD sensitization).

OVERLAP common; histology often indistinguishable.

questions.stem: You need to treat this patient with PPD allergy.
questions.question: How is contact dermatitis treated?

options.text: Antihistamines are first-line
Continued exposure is acceptable

CORNERSTONE: Allergen/irritant AVOIDANCE - most important!; Patient education on sources and cross-reactants; ACUTE/LOCALIZED: Mid-high potency topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone 0.1%, clobetasol 0.05%); EXTENSIVE (>20% BSA): Systemic corticosteroids - taper over 2-3 weeks for rhus dermatitis; Emollients for barrier repair; Cool compresses for acute vesicular; For refractory: Consider phototherapy, immunosuppressants
Oral steroids for all cases

questions.explanation: Treatment of contact dermatitis: MOST IMPORTANT: AVOIDANCE of causative allergen/irritant! Without avoidance, treatment will fail. Patient education on: All sources of allergen, Cross-reactive substances, Safe alternative products. ACUTE/LOCALIZED: Topical corticosteroids: Mid-potency (triamcinolone 0.1%) for body, High-potency (clobetasol 0.05%) for hands/feet (thick skin), Low-potency for face/folds. Cool compresses for acute weeping. Barrier repair with emollients. EXTENSIVE/SEVERE (>20% BSA): Systemic corticosteroids: Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day, TAPER over 2-3 weeks for poison ivy (rapid taper → rebound!). CHRONIC/REFRACTORY: Tacrolimus/pimecrolimus (steroid-sparing, good for face). Phototherapy (narrowband UVB, PUVA). Systemic immunosuppression rarely needed. This patient: Avoid PPD, provide list of safe hair dye alternatives, topical steroids for hands, consider gloves.

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient needs to continue working as a hairdresser.
questions.question: What avoidance and protective strategies should be recommended?

options.text: For PPD allergy: Avoid permanent/semi-permanent hair dyes containing PPD; Avoid temporary henna tattoos (often contain PPD); Cross-reactants: PABA (sunscreens), some azo dyes, benzocaine, sulfonamides; PROTECTIVE measures: Wear nitrile gloves (not latex) when handling dyes; Barrier creams before work; Frequent emollient application; Minimize wet work duration; Provide written list of safe alternatives
Latex gloves are preferred protection
Avoidance is impossible for hairdressers
No cross-reactants exist

questions.explanation: Avoidance and protection strategies for PPD allergy: PPD AVOIDANCE: Avoid permanent and semi-permanent hair dyes containing PPD or PTD (paratoluenediamine). Avoid black henna temporary tattoos (often contain PPD). Read ingredient labels carefully. CROSS-REACTIVE SUBSTANCES: PABA (para-aminobenzoic acid) in some sunscreens, Benzocaine (topical anesthetic), Procaine, Some sulfonamides, Azo dyes (in textiles, foods), Rubber chemicals. WORKPLACE PROTECTION: Nitrile or vinyl gloves (not latex - different allergen, not protective against PPD). Change gloves frequently. Barrier creams before exposure. Good ventilation. Minimize wet work. Frequent emollient use. PATIENT EDUCATION: Written list of safe products, Cross-reactant list, Contact resources (ACDS CAMP database).

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This hairdresser has occupational allergic contact dermatitis to PPD.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Oral antihistamines alone
Change career is the only option
Empiric treatment without patch testing

Systematic approach: (1) Confirm diagnosis: Chronic hand dermatitis, occupational exposure, improves on vacation → suspect ACD; (2) Patch testing: Comprehensive series including hairdresser allergens → PPD positive (++), crescendo pattern; (3) Establish relevance: DEFINITE - exposure + distribution + improvement with avoidance; (4) Treatment: High-potency topical steroids for hands + emollients; (5) Avoidance counseling: PPD sources, cross-reactants, safe alternatives; (6) Workplace modifications: Nitrile gloves, barrier creams, ventilation; (7) Follow-up to ensure clearance

questions.explanation: Complete management for this patient: DIAGNOSIS: (1) Clinical suspicion: Chronic hand dermatitis, occupational hairdresser, improves on vacation. (2) Patch testing with standard series + occupational (hairdresser) series. (3) Result: PPD ++ reaction, crescendo pattern 48→96h. (4) Clinical relevance: DEFINITE (confirmed exposure, compatible distribution, improves with avoidance). TREATMENT: (1) Acute: High-potency topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05%) for hands BID x 2 weeks. (2) Maintenance: Emollients frequently, barrier repair. (3) Consider tacrolimus for long-term steroid-sparing. AVOIDANCE COUNSELING: (1) Written list of PPD-containing products to avoid. (2) Cross-reactant list (PABA, benzocaine, etc.). (3) Safe alternative hair dye products without PPD. WORKPLACE MODIFICATIONS: Nitrile gloves, barrier creams, good ventilation, minimize wet work. FOLLOW-UP: Reassess in 4-6 weeks for resolution. If no improvement → reassess exposures and diagnosis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Contact Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected contact dermatitis?

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in contact dermatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in contact dermatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in contact dermatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

B.misconception: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
B.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of contact dermatitis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

A.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: When managing contact dermatitis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

B.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.
C.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization.
